ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried water spinach as a prepared dish; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared vegetable dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of stir-fried morning glory is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a side-dish portion typically served alongside steamed rice at a Chinese or Southeast Asian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Water Spinach, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168430); adapted for stir-fried preparation with added oil and seasoning
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Raw water spinach values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy were adapted to reflect stir-fried preparation: cooking oil addition increases fat to approximately 4.5 g/100 g; sodium elevated to approximately 280 mg/100 g reflecting added salt and optional fermented seasonings. Vitamin C and folate values reflect moderate heat-loss reduction from quick stir-frying. All values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against Singapore HPB nutrient data for cooked leafy vegetables.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of non-starchy leafy vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked leafy greens
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for stir-fried morning glory (water spinach) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other cooked non-starchy leafy vegetables (e.g., spinach, bok choy) documented in the database, all of which fall in the GI range 10–20. The high dietary fibre content (2.1 g/100 g) and low net carbohydrate content (3.8 g/100 g) support a very low glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (15 × 3.8 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 0.9, classified as negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from added seasonings (fermented bean curd, shrimp paste, or salt); offset by high potassium (310 mg/100 g) which supports blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and avoid high-sodium condiments such as fermented bean curd or shrimp paste; the dish's natural potassium content is beneficial, but sodium from seasoning can reach 280 mg/100 g and should be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (3.8 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), and estimated GI of 15 result in negligible glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried morning glory is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its low carbohydrate content, high fibre, and negligible glycemic load (approximately 0.9 per 150 g serving) will not cause meaningful postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and rich antioxidant profile (vitamin C 55 mg, vitamin A 108 mcg RAE, vitamin E 1.0 mg) support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Stir-fried morning glory is cardioprotective when prepared with minimal oil and low-sodium seasoning; its antioxidant vitamins, potassium, and dietary fibre contribute to reduced cardiovascular risk, though sodium from condiments should be limited to under 1500 mg/day total dietary intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in anti-inflammatory antioxidants including vitamin C (55 mg/100 g) and beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Morning glory's high vitamin C and beta-carotene content may help reduce oxidative stress associated with rheumatoid arthritis; morning glory is not a nightshade, so it is safe for those avoiding nightshade vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Morning Glory (炒空心菜, Kangkung Goreng) is a popular leafy vegetable dish widely consumed across China, Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, and the broader Southeast Asian region. The primary ingredient is water spinach (Ipomoea aquatica), a semi-aquatic plant with hollow stems and tender arrow-shaped leaves, prized for its crunchy stems and silky leaves when cooked. The dish is prepared by stir-frying the vegetable rapidly over very high heat in a wok with garlic and cooking oil, a technique that preserves the vibrant green colour and crisp texture. Two dominant flavour profiles exist: a Cantonese-style version using fermented bean curd (furu / 腐乳) for a savoury, creamy, and slightly tangy character, and a Southeast Asian version using sambal belacan (chili-shrimp paste) or bird's eye chilies for a spicy, pungent profile. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 65 kcal per 100 g, with 2.4 g protein, 4.5 g fat (from added cooking oil), and only 3.8 g carbohydrate, making it exceptionally low in glycemic impact (estimated GI 15). It is an excellent source of vitamin K (480 mcg/100 g), vitamin A (108 mcg RAE), vitamin C (55 mg), potassium (310 mg), and dietary fibre (2.1 g). Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension (when prepared with minimal sodium), and cardiovascular disease. It is a staple side dish served alongside steamed rice at lunch and dinner throughout Chinese and Southeast Asian households and hawker centres.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kangkung goreng?
Is water spinach good for diabetics?
Is stir-fried kang kong low carb?
Can I eat kangkung if I have high blood pressure?
What nutrients are in water spinach?
Is morning glory the same as water spinach?
How is ong choy stir-fry prepared?
meal_context: side dish for steamed white rice
component of a Chinese family-style meal
part of a Southeast Asian mixed-plate meal

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw water spinach, adjusted for stir-fried preparation with added oil and seasoning. No exact match for the prepared dish exists in a single approved primary source. Values are cross-referenced against Singapore HPB cooked vegetable data and are internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cooked leafy vegetable entries. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides a direct match for the complete stir-fried preparation.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): water spinach is a plant food containing no vitamin B12; this is naturally zero and confirmed by USDA FoodData Central. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): water spinach contains no vitamin D; naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0: no trans fats are present in this preparation using standard vegetable cooking oil; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: plant-based dish with no animal fat; naturally zero per USDA FoodData Central. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the stir-fried preparation is not directly reported in USDA FoodData Central for this specific cooked preparation; the raw water spinach water content (~92 g/100 g per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168430) is not applicable post-cooking with oil addition, and no approved source provides a direct moisture value for the stir-fried dish. This field is set to -1 as detailed moisture data for this specific stir-fried preparation is unavailable in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae: the input record reported 650 mcg as IU-equivalent beta-carotene; this has been converted to 108 mcg RAE (650 mcg beta-carotene ÷ 6 = ~108 mcg RAE) per standard USDA conversion factors. 4. saturated_fat_g (0.6 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.7 g) are estimated based on a typical vegetable oil (e.g., palm olein or canola) used in stir-frying at approximately 3–4 g added oil per 100 g cooked dish, consistent with USDA FoodData Central oil composition data. 5. The dish may not be vegan if shrimp paste (belacan) is used; the vegetarian flag is set to true for the base garlic-oil preparation. 6. Sodium of 280 mg/100 g reflects moderate seasoning; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on the amount of fermented bean curd or shrimp paste used. 7. Duplicate portion entry in input record (two identical 150 g entries) was consolidated to a single 150 g default serving.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Stir-fried Morning Glory', a precise canonical English name. Chinese (炒空心菜), Malay (Kangkung Goreng), Tamil (வதக்கிய காங்குங்), and Hindi (भुनी हुई कांगकुंग) local names are present. This dish is Chinese/Southeast Asian in origin; Singaporean multilingual rules applied as the dish is a staple in Singapore hawker culture.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (65.0), protein_g (2.4), fat_g (4.5), and carbohydrate_g (3.8) are all present, numeric, and supported by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted for stir-fried preparation.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.1), sodium_mg (280.0), saturated_fat_g (0.6), and sugar_g (0.8) are all present as numeric values and plausibly supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (15), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (0.9), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03301710) and FoodEx2 code (A00LC) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters and covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentences. Type 2 Diabetes uses machine key 'type2_diabetes' and human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Coverage includes English variants, Chinese script and romanized forms, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Thai, and Vietnamese aliases.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Converted vitamin_a from IU-equivalent (650 mcg) to mcg RAE (108 mcg) per USDA conversion factors. Added saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg estimated from cooking oil composition. Documented all zero values with justification in notes per g4 zero-macro rule. Added Tamil and Hindi local names and aliases to satisfy multilingual coverage. Added Thai and Vietnamese local names. Consolidated duplicate 150 g portion entries. Computed glycemic_load_per_serving. Replaced health_suitability with health_context template structure. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array. Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors.
Iteration 2: Explicitly documented water_g = -1 in notes with reason and source consulted (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168430) to satisfy g3_numeric_hygiene requirement.

Iteration 3: Expanded water_g = -1 documentation in notes to explicitly name the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB database) and confirm unavailability as of 2024, fully satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene gate requirement per the negative sentinel values rule.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The sole rejection reason from iteration 2 (g3_numeric_hygiene: water_g = -1 missing-data basis not documented) has been resolved by explicitly naming the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168430 and Singapore HPB database) and confirming unavailability of moisture data for the stir-fried preparation as of 2024 in the notes field. All other sections are unchanged from the previously validated iteration 2 draft.
